rationale: Standard finite difference with moderate grid and 2nd order accuracy is robust and efficient, but accuracy is limited for a 4th order PDE. Suitable for basic use, but not optimal for high-accuracy needs.
High-order finite difference on a fine grid with an efficient iterative solver provides high accuracy and good stability for the biharmonic problem, though at increased computational cost.
Quadratic FEM is well-suited for biharmonic problems and clamped BCs, offering good stability and flexibility. Accuracy is moderate due to P2 elements, and cost is reasonable.
Linear FEM on a fine mesh is robust but less accurate per DOF for a 4th order PDE. Computational cost is high relative to accuracy, making it less optimal.
Spectral Chebyshev methods are nearly optimal for smooth solutions on simple domains with clamped BCs, offering extremely high accuracy and stability at moderate cost.
Fourier spectral methods are efficient and accurate for periodic problems, but the clamped BCs here are incompatible, making this plan unsuitable despite high formal accuracy.
Finite volume methods are less natural for biharmonic (4th order) problems, leading to lower accuracy and potential implementation complexity, though stability and efficiency are reasonable.
Mixed FEM can handle biharmonic equations and clamped BCs well, but P1/P0 elements limit accuracy. Stability is good and cost is moderate.
Coarse grid and low-order finite difference yield poor accuracy for a 4th order PDE, though the method is stable and very cheap computationally.
hp-adaptive FEM is highly suitable for biharmonic problems, offering excellent accuracy and efficiency via adaptivity, though at high implementation and computational cost.
